children.name: Numenius
children.description: Curlews — large, long-billed waders whose haunting, liquid calls are among the most evocative sounds in the natural world. The Eurasian curlew is one of Sweden's most recognisable waders, its long decurved bill perfectly adapted for probing into mud, peat, and grass root zones to extract earthworms, insects, and crustaceans. The bubbling song of the curlew, delivered in soaring display flight over boggy farmland and coastal meadows, is the defining sound of the Swedish countryside in spring — but it is a sound that is becoming scarcer as agricultural intensification and drainage destroy the wet grasslands the curlew requires for nesting.

children.name: Numenius arquata
children.description: The largest wader in Europe and one of its most urgently declining breeding birds, its long downcurved bill can reach 15 cm in females. The fluty, bubbling song — rising into a cascade of liquid notes — is the quintessential sound of Swedish coastal meadows and boggy farmland in spring. Breeding numbers have halved since the 1980s due to agricultural drainage and predator pressure; Swedish west coast meadows and the larger lake systems are key breeding strongholds.

children.name: Numenius madagascariensis
children.description: The world's largest shorebird, breeding in marshy grasslands of eastern Siberia and northeastern China before undertaking one of the longest migrations of any wader to wintering grounds in Australia. Its bill can exceed 20 cm and is used to probe deeply into the soft sediments of estuaries for crabs and other invertebrates. Listed as Endangered, the species has suffered severe population declines due to loss of Yellow Sea intertidal staging habitats.

children.name: Numenius americanus
children.description: North America's largest shorebird, with a spectacular sickle-shaped bill reaching up to 21 cm in females. Breeds on dry and wet grasslands of western North America and winters on estuaries and agricultural fields along both coasts of Mexico and Central America. The cinnamon-buff plumage and loud, rising 'cur-lee' call make it instantly recognisable in its arid breeding habitats.

children.name: Numenius phaeopus
children.description: A smaller, more widespread curlew with a strongly striped crown and a shorter, kinked bill. It breeds on subarctic and arctic tundra across the Northern Hemisphere and is the most cosmopolitan of the curlews, migrating to virtually every tropical and temperate coast in the world. The rippling, bubbling trill — seven notes descending in pitch — is one of the most characteristic sounds of open moorland and upland bogs in summer.

children.name: Numenius hudsonicus
children.description: Recently split from N. phaeopus as a distinct species, this North American form breeds on subarctic tundra around Hudson Bay and on the tundra of northern Alaska and Canada. It lacks the white rump patch seen in the Eurasian whimbrel, appearing dark-rumped in flight. Winters along the Pacific coast of South America from Ecuador to Chile, undertaking a remarkable transoceanic migration.

children.name: Numenius tahitiensis
children.description: A remarkable Pacific curlew that breeds on remote mountain tundra in western Alaska and winters on Pacific island atolls and reefs, undertaking a non-stop oceanic crossing of several thousand kilometres. Uniquely among waders, it is known to become flightless during moult on its wintering islands, making it vulnerable to introduced predators. The stiff, bristle-like thigh feathers visible at close range give the species its distinctive name.

children.name: Numenius minutus
children.description: The smallest curlew, barely larger than a godwit, with a relatively short and only moderately decurved bill. Breeds in dry larch and birch forest clearings in central and eastern Siberia, and winters on grasslands and floodplains in northern Australia. Despite its small size it performs a remarkable migration between Siberia and Australia, staging in large numbers on the plains of New South Wales and Queensland.

children.name: Numenius borealis
children.description: Almost certainly extinct, this small curlew was once one of the most abundant shorebirds in North America, breeding on arctic tundra in Canada and wintering on the pampas of Argentina. Relentless market hunting during the 19th century and loss of grassland habitats drove it to the brink; the last confirmed record was in 1963 in Barbados. Despite occasional unconfirmed reports, no verified sighting has occurred in decades and the species is almost certainly gone forever.

children.name: Numenius tenuirostris
children.description: Critically Endangered and almost certainly extinct, this small curlew bred in boggy forest clearings of central Siberia and wintered around the Mediterranean. Historically wintered in North Africa, Italy, and the Levant; the last confirmed records date from the 1990s near Merja Zerga in Morocco, and no verified sightings have occurred since. Slightly smaller and slimmer-billed than the Eurasian curlew, distinguished also by distinctive round dark spots on the flanks. Its disappearance remains one of the greatest conservation failures in European ornithology.

children.name: Limosa
children.description: Godwits — large waders with long, straight or slightly upturned bills, named from the Old English for 'good' and a word for their cry. Four species constitute this genus, and all are renowned for extraordinary long-distance migrations. The bar-tailed godwit holds the world record for non-stop bird flight, covering over 12,000 km from Alaska to New Zealand without landing. In breeding plumage godwits are strikingly coloured in rich chestnut and brick red; in winter they adopt plain grey-brown tones. Several species have experienced steep population declines due to loss of intertidal habitats at staging sites.

children.name: Limosa limosa
children.description: A tall, elegant wader with a striking black-and-white tail pattern visible in flight and a long straight bicoloured bill. Breeds in wet meadows and boggy grasslands from Iceland and the British Isles across Europe and central Asia. One of the most threatened waders in Europe, with many western populations in serious decline due to agricultural intensification. The islandica subspecies breeding in Iceland winters largely in Britain and Ireland, while limosa breeds across continental Europe and migrates to sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Limosa lapponica
children.description: The champion long-distance migrant of the bird world, performing the longest non-stop flight recorded for any bird — over 12,000 km from Alaska to New Zealand without stopping for food or water. Before departure birds double their weight in fat reserves and their digestive organs shrink to make room for enlarged flight muscles. Breeds on arctic tundra from Scandinavia to Alaska; the baueri subspecies of Alaska makes the incredible trans-Pacific crossing to Australia and New Zealand.

children.name: Limosa haemastica
children.description: A medium-sized godwit of the Americas that breeds on subarctic tundra in western and central Canada and migrates in a spectacular elliptical pattern: south across the Atlantic to the Pampas of Argentina in autumn, returning via the Great Plains in spring. In breeding plumage the deep chestnut underparts with bold barring are striking. Long considered rare, surveys have revealed larger populations than previously thought, though it remains a species of conservation concern.

children.name: Limosa fedoa
children.description: The largest godwit in the Americas, identifiable in all plumages by its cinnamon-buff and dark brown marbled upperparts and the distinctive orange-buff base of its long, slightly upturned bill. Breeds on short-grass prairies and wet meadows of central North America and winters on Pacific and Gulf coast estuaries from California and the southern US to Colombia and Peru. Flocks on wintering estuaries can number in the hundreds, probing the soft sediment for worms and bivalves.

children.name: Gallinago
children.description: Snipe — cryptically streaked marsh birds whose remarkable aerial displays produce one of the most unusual sounds in the animal kingdom. The common snipe is a master of camouflage, its brown-and-buff streaked plumage rendering it almost invisible among sedge and rush as it stands motionless. When flushed it explodes from underfoot in a fast, erratic, zigzagging flight that historically made it the most challenging of all quarry species for hunters — giving rise to the word 'sniper'. In spring, male snipe perform 'drumming' or 'bleating' display dives over their territories: as the bird stoops at speed, the two outermost tail feathers spread and vibrate in the airstream to produce a distinctive pulsing sound audible for hundreds of metres.

children.name: Gallinago gallinago
children.description: A widespread but declining breeder in bogs, marshes, and wet meadows across the Holarctic. Its bill, one of the longest relative to body size of any bird, is sensitive at the tip, allowing it to detect prey by touch deep in soft mud. The spring drumming display — a distinctive bleating produced by vibrating outer tail feathers during a swooping dive — is heard on breeding grounds from April to July across the boreal zone.

children.name: Gallinago hardwickii
children.description: A large snipe breeding in the highlands of Hokkaido and Honshu in Japan, undertaking a remarkable direct southward migration to winter in eastern Australia — one of the longest migrations of any snipe. Named after Thomas Hardwicke, British Army general and naturalist who collected specimens in Asia in the early 19th century. It favours wet grasslands, marshes, and rice paddies, and is heavily dependent on Japanese highland bogs for breeding.

children.name: Gallinago stenura
children.description: Named for its remarkable narrow outer tail feathers — pin-like projections that are invisible in the field but diagnostic when a bird is in the hand. Breeds in boggy forest clearings and tundra margins across Siberia and central Asia; winters in South and Southeast Asia. In the field it is very similar to common snipe, but its narrower wing and subtler supercilium are useful features. The pin-tail feathers produce a softer aerial display sound than the common snipe.

children.name: Gallinago megala
children.description: Named after Robert Swinhoe, the 19th-century British consul and naturalist who described many East Asian birds. A large snipe breeding in boggy grasslands across central and eastern Siberia, migrating to winter in Southeast Asia and northern Australia. Extremely difficult to distinguish from pin-tailed snipe in the field; best told by the number and arrangement of outer tail feathers, which are narrower and more numerous than in gallinago.

children.name: Gallinago nigripennis
children.description: A resident snipe of sub-Saharan Africa, found in montane bogs, marshes, and wet grasslands from Ethiopia and the highlands of East Africa south to the Cape. It closely resembles the common snipe but is non-migratory, resident in its high-altitude grassland habitats year-round. The drumming display is similar to that of the common snipe, performed over open boggy areas at dawn and dusk.

children.name: Gallinago macrodactyla
children.description: An endemic resident of Madagascar, found in marshes, rice paddies, and wet grasslands, mainly in the eastern half of the island and in highland areas. One of the least-known snipe species, it has rarely been studied in the field and much of its biology remains poorly understood. Its large feet, suggested by the name macrodactyla, may be an adaptation for moving through dense aquatic vegetation in the island's wetland habitats.

children.name: Gallinago media
children.description: A larger and bulkier snipe with heavily barred underparts and prominent white outer tail feathers, performing one of the most spectacular lek displays of any European wader. Males gather at traditional lek sites in Scandinavian and Russian bogs at night during May and June, where they jostle for position, spread their tails to display white feathers, and produce mechanical rattling and clicking sounds. The species is declining throughout its range due to loss of wet grassland habitats.

children.name: Gallinago undulata
children.description: The world's largest snipe, a secretive resident of wet grasslands and marshes across northern South America from Venezuela and Trinidad south to Argentina. Despite its size it is seldom seen, preferring dense grass cover and only flushing reluctantly; when it does flush it reveals striking barred and spotted plumage and a remarkably slow, direct flight for a snipe. It performs aerial drumming displays similar to those of the common snipe.

children.name: Gallinago jamesoni
children.description: A high-altitude snipe resident in Andean bogs and paramo grasslands from Venezuela and Colombia south to Peru, typically found above 2,500 m elevation. Named after William Jameson, a 19th-century British botanist who collected natural history specimens in Ecuador. A poorly known species rarely observed in its high-altitude habitats; little studied by ornithologists due to the remoteness and difficulty of its range.

children.name: Gallinago stricklandii
children.description: A resident snipe of the southernmost tip of South America, found in Tierra del Fuego, the Falkland Islands, and southern Chile and Argentina. Named after Hugh Edwin Strickland, 19th-century British ornithologist and geologist. One of the southernmost-distributed snipe in the world, it inhabits boggy grasslands and the margins of Magellanic moorland. Its population is small and it is classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Gallinago imperialis
children.description: One of the rarest and least-known snipe in the world, known from only a handful of specimens and a very small number of modern observations in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Inhabits cloud forest edges and highland bogs above 2,000 m. Its dark, heavily patterned plumage, large size, and extremely elusive habits make it very difficult to study. Listed as Endangered due to its tiny known range and ongoing habitat loss from deforestation.

children.name: Gallinago nemoricola
children.description: A large, dark-plumaged snipe of high-altitude forests and meadows in the Himalayas, breeding from Afghanistan east to southwestern China. Unlike most snipe which favour open marshy habitats, the wood snipe is strongly associated with montane forest clearings and dense undergrowth near streams. It descends to lower elevations in winter, wintering in the plains of India and Southeast Asia. A poorly known and relatively scarce species classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Gallinago solitaria
children.description: A large, pale snipe found in mountain streams, bogs, and wet scrublands from the Urals and the Himalayas east to Japan and the Russian Far East. More solitary than most snipe species, it is typically found singly or in very small numbers at mountain watercourses with stony margins and overhanging vegetation. Partially migratory, descending from montane breeding grounds to lowland rivers and marshes in winter.

children.name: Gallinago andina
children.description: A resident of high-altitude puna grasslands and bogs in the Andes of Peru, Bolivia, and northern Chile and Argentina, typically found above 4,000 m elevation. One of the highest-altitude breeding snipe in the world, inhabiting the harsh treeless grasslands of the altiplano. Little-studied and poorly known; its biology and population size remain inadequately surveyed. Sometimes considered a subspecies of Gallinago paraguaiae.

children.name: Gallinago paraguaiae
children.description: The most widespread snipe of South America, found from Venezuela and Colombia south through Brazil, Paraguay, and Argentina to Patagonia. Inhabits a variety of wetland habitats including marshes, wet grasslands, rice paddies, and the margins of rivers and lakes from sea level to moderate altitudes. The most familiar snipe encountered by birders throughout the South American continent, performing aerial display flights similar to those of the common snipe.

children.name: Gallinago nobilis
children.description: A large snipe of Andean paramo and boggy grasslands in Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, and Peru, typically occurring above 2,000 m elevation. One of the more poorly known South American snipe, it inhabits high-altitude wet grasslands and bogs in the eastern and central Andes. Like other Andean snipe it performs aerial drumming displays and is cryptically camouflaged against the tussock grasses and sedges of its paramo habitat.

children.name: Gallinago delicata
children.description: The common snipe of North America, formerly considered conspecific with Gallinago gallinago. Named in honour of Alexander Wilson, the pioneering Scottish-American ornithologist considered the father of American ornithology. Breeds in wet meadows, marshes, and bogs across Canada and the northern United States; winters south to Central America and northern South America. The winnowing display flight — produced by outer tail feathers vibrating in the air — is one of the characteristic sounds of North American wetlands in spring.

children.name: Lymnocryptes
children.description: Jack snipe — a monotypic genus containing only the jack snipe, the smallest member of the Scolopacidae and one of the most secretive and camouflaged of all waders. Sometimes included within Gallinago, Lymnocryptes is treated as distinct based on its unique morphology, behaviour, and molecular data. The jack snipe has a distinctly different tail structure from Gallinago snipe, with a pointed central tail and different outer tail feather morphology. Its extreme reluctance to flush and habit of 'freezing' motionless in vegetation has made it one of the most challenging waders to study.

children.name: Lymnocryptes minima
children.description: The world's smallest snipe and one of its most cryptic birds, relying entirely on camouflage and freezing behaviour rather than flight for predator avoidance. Breeds in boggy subarctic forest from Scandinavia to Siberia; winters south to tropical Africa and South Asia. Males perform a remarkable ground display resembling the galloping of a horse — a repetitive four-beat sound produced mechanically — giving rise to one of its folk names 'horse snipe'. The bouncing feeding action caused by compressed body movements is highly distinctive.

children.name: Coenocorypha
children.description: New Zealand snipe — a genus of small, cryptic snipe endemic to the remote islands of New Zealand and its subantarctic territories. These birds evolved in isolation following colonisation of the southwestern Pacific by a Gallinago ancestor, diverging into several island-specific forms. Coenocorypha snipe are characterised by their extremely short wings and almost flightless habits on their predator-free island homes; several island populations were extirpated following the introduction of rats and other predators. The surviving species perform remarkable nocturnal aerial display flights, producing sounds with their outer tail feathers — a behaviour paralleling the 'drumming' of their Gallinago relatives.

children.name: Coenocorypha aucklandica
children.description: A small, cryptically patterned snipe of the New Zealand subantarctic islands, with subspecies distributed across the Auckland, Antipodes, Bounty, and Snares island groups. Each island group hosts its own genetically distinct population, the result of long isolation on these remote, windswept outposts. Rats have extirpated Coenocorypha snipe from many islands where they once occurred; surviving populations on rat-free islands remain important targets for conservation management. Performs an extraordinary 'hakawai' aerial display at night, plunging at speed with tail feathers spread.

children.name: Coenocorypha pusilla
children.description: A small snipe endemic to the Chatham Islands group east of New Zealand, where it survives on a few rat-free offshore islets after being extirpated from all the main islands. One of the most threatened waders in the world, its total population numbers only in the hundreds. Conservation translocations to additional pest-free islands have been undertaken to establish new insurance populations. Like the subantarctic snipe it performs nocturnal aerial displays, diving at speed and producing a loud 'pumping' sound with its tail feathers.

children.name: Coenocorypha huegeli
children.description: Named after Charles von Hügel, the 19th-century Austrian explorer and naturalist who travelled extensively in South Asia and the Pacific. A snipe of the Pitt Island group within the Chatham Islands archipelago, distinguished from the Chatham Islands snipe by its larger size and slightly different morphology. Like other Coenocorypha snipe it was once more widespread but has been severely impacted by introduced predators; current population status is poorly documented due to the remoteness of its island range.

children.name: Scolopax
children.description: Woodcocks — the most terrestrial of the sandpipers, inhabiting damp woodland and forest rather than open wetlands. The Eurasian woodcock is one of the most enigmatic birds in the temperate Holarctic: its cryptic dead-leaf plumage makes it essentially invisible on the forest floor, and it is almost exclusively nocturnal. The one exception is the spectacular 'roding' display flight performed by males at dusk and dawn throughout spring: the male follows a regular circuit over the woodland canopy, uttering a series of deep grunting 'orr-orr-orr' sounds alternating with a high squeaky 'twick'. Woodcocks have eyes positioned high on the sides of their head, giving 360-degree vision — a complete field of view without turning their head.

children.name: Scolopax rusticola
children.description: A secretive woodland wader of damp mixed forest throughout the Palearctic. Almost never seen on the ground in daylight, relying on perfect camouflage to remain invisible among leaf litter. The evening roding display flight — a regular patrol over the treetops with its unique grunting and squeaking calls — is one of Europe's most anticipated spring wildlife experiences. Eyes set high and to the rear of the head provide complete 360-degree vision for predator detection while the bill probes vertically into soil.

children.name: Scolopax saturata
children.description: A dark, richly coloured woodcock resident in montane forests of Sumatra and Java, where it inhabits dense primary and secondary forest above 1,500 m elevation. The most deeply rufous of the Scolopax woodcocks, its warm chestnut plumage provides perfect camouflage among the dark forest leaf litter of its montane habitat. Very little studied in the field; much of its biology and behaviour remains poorly documented.

children.name: Scolopax rosenbergii
children.description: Named after Hermann von Rosenberg, the 19th-century Dutch explorer and naturalist who collected extensively in New Guinea and the Moluccas. Endemic to the mountains of New Guinea, where it inhabits dense montane forest from 1,500 m to over 3,000 m elevation. One of the least-known woodcocks, with very few specimens and observations available; its ecology and behaviour are almost entirely undocumented. Likely performs roding display flights similar to those of the Eurasian woodcock.

children.name: Scolopax bukidnonensis
children.description: Endemic to the island of Mindanao in the Philippines, named after the Bukidnon plateau where specimens were first collected. Inhabits montane forest in the highlands of Mindanao above 1,000 m elevation; one of the Philippines' most range-restricted endemic birds. Almost entirely unknown in life; virtually nothing is documented about its behaviour, ecology, or breeding biology. Listed as Vulnerable due to ongoing deforestation in its restricted range.

children.name: Scolopax celebensis
children.description: Endemic to the island of Sulawesi (formerly Celebes) in Indonesia, where it inhabits montane forest above 1,000 m elevation. One of the island-endemic Scolopax woodcocks of Wallacea and the Philippines, all of which evolved in isolation from the Eurasian woodcock and are poorly known. Very rarely observed and almost nothing is documented about its breeding biology or ecology. Named for Celebes, the former name for Sulawesi.

children.name: Scolopax rochussenii
children.description: Named after Jan Jacob Rochussen, Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies in the 1840s. Restricted to the islands of Obi and Bacan in the North Moluccas of Indonesia, making it one of the most range-restricted of all woodcocks. Known from fewer than two dozen specimens; no live observations of any quality have been published and its ecology is entirely unstudied. Listed as Vulnerable due to its tiny range and deforestation pressure.

children.name: Scolopax mira
children.description: Endemic to the Amami Islands of southern Japan, where it is a rare and legally protected resident. Inhabits dense subtropical forest on Amami-Oshima and a few neighbouring islands; the species name mira means 'wonderful' or 'remarkable'. It is nocturnal and very rarely seen; population estimates suggest only a few hundred individuals remain. Classified as Vulnerable and one of Japan's most threatened endemic birds, at risk from habitat loss and predation by introduced mongooses and feral cats.

children.name: Scolopax minor
children.description: The North American equivalent of the Eurasian woodcock, famous for the male's remarkable spring sky dance: at dusk males call from the ground with loud 'peent' notes, then spiral up into the sky before tumbling back down in a zigzagging flight, wings twittering. A bird of young forest and alder thickets of eastern North America; a game species whose populations have declined significantly due to loss of early-successional forest habitat. Like the Eurasian woodcock, it probes deep into moist soil for earthworms.

children.name: Scolopax javaensis
children.description: A poorly known woodcock endemic to the island of Java in Indonesia, inhabiting montane forest at elevations above 1,000 m in the volcanic highland interior. One of the least-studied of all woodcocks; very few confirmed observations exist and most of what is known about the species comes from specimens collected in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Like other island-endemic Scolopax woodcocks, it is classified as Vulnerable due to ongoing deforestation in its restricted range.

children.name: Scolopax scutulata
children.description: A large, dark woodcock of montane forest in the Greater Sundas and adjacent islands of Indonesia, closely related to the Eurasian woodcock but considerably larger and darker in plumage. Inhabits dense primary and secondary montane forest above 1,200 m in Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines. One of the more imposing woodcock species but poorly known due to its nocturnal habits and dense forest habitat; its roding display flights have rarely been described.

children.name: Tringa
children.description: Shanks, tattlers, and their allies — medium-sized, long-legged waders of wetland edges and mudflats that are among the most familiar waders in the Holarctic. The group contains some of the most abundant breeding waders in Scandinavia (redshank, wood sandpiper) as well as common passage migrants. Tringa sandpipers are characteristically nervous and alert birds — the redshank in particular functions as a sentinel species in European wetlands, its loud, piping alarm calls instantly alerting every bird in the area to potential predators. Many Tringa species breed in Scandinavia and pass through Europe in large numbers during autumn migration, when estuary mudflats and lake shores can be covered in feeding flocks.

children.name: Tringa totanus
children.description: A loud, nervous sentinel of coastal meadows, marshes, and lakeshores, its bright orange-red bill base and legs and distinctive white trailing edge to the wing in flight make it unmistakeable. Breeds in wet grasslands and coastal meadows throughout the Palearctic; a declining species due to drainage. Its penetrating, yelping alarm calls are so effective that virtually every other wader in the vicinity responds instantly — earning it the nickname 'the warden of the marshes'.

children.name: Tringa erythropus
children.description: A long-legged, elegant wader that is strikingly different in breeding and non-breeding plumage: in summer the adult is almost entirely sooty-black with white spotting, one of the most dramatic summer plumages of any Palearctic wader. Breeds in boreal and subarctic forest bogs from Scandinavia east to Siberia; winters in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and around the Mediterranean. The long red legs dangling in flight and the distinctive 'tchuit' call are useful identification features.

children.name: Tringa nebularia
children.description: A large, elegant wader with a slightly upturned grey-green bill and loud, ringing three-note call. Breeds in open boggy forests in northern Scandinavia and across the boreal zone; a common passage migrant south through Europe. Feeding birds wade actively in shallow water, sometimes chasing fish with surprising speed and agility. Its call — one of the most characteristic sounds of a northern bog in June — carries far across open moorland.

children.name: Tringa stagnatilis
children.description: A slender, long-legged wader resembling a miniature greenshank, with a needle-fine straight bill and particularly delicate proportions. Breeds in wet steppe grasslands and the margins of lakes and rivers across central Asia from eastern Europe to China. Winters in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Australia, often feeding in shallow freshwater pools and flooded rice fields. A graceful, active feeder that picks delicately from the water surface with its fine bill.

children.name: Tringa flavipes
children.description: A slender, long-legged North American wader with bright yellow legs and a straight, needle-fine bill, very similar to the greater yellowlegs but noticeably smaller and with a one- or two-note call rather than three or four. Breeds in open boreal forest clearings and bogs across Canada and Alaska; a common passage migrant throughout the Americas, wintering from the southern US to Tierra del Fuego. Feeds actively in shallow water, picking small invertebrates from the surface.

children.name: Tringa melanoleuca
children.description: A large, boldly marked wader with bright yellow legs and a slightly upturned bill that is one of the most familiar shorebirds of North American wetlands during migration. Breeds in boreal muskegs and boggy forest clearings across Canada; a common and widespread migrant throughout the Americas, wintering from the southern US to Patagonia. Feeds energetically, often wading belly-deep and sweeping its bill through the water to catch small fish and invertebrates.

children.name: Tringa semipalmata
children.description: A large, stocky North American wader cryptically grey-brown at rest but strikingly patterned in flight with a bold black-and-white wing pattern. The loud, ringing 'pill-will-willet' call is one of the most characteristic sounds of North American coastal marshes and beaches. The eastern subspecies semipalmatus breeds in salt marshes of the Atlantic coast while the western inornatus breeds in prairie grasslands and wetlands; the two populations are so different in size and habitat that they may represent separate species.

children.name: Tringa solitaria
children.description: A dark-backed, white-spectacled sandpiper that is unusual among North American Tringa in that it lays its eggs in abandoned tree nests of other birds (particularly thrushes) rather than on the ground. Breeds in boreal forest across Canada and Alaska; a passage migrant throughout the Americas in autumn, found at freshwater pools, forest streams, and marshes. When alarmed it bobs the front of its body up and down — the opposite to the tail-bobbing of the common sandpiper.

children.name: Tringa ochropus
children.description: A dark, stocky sandpiper with strongly contrasting dark upperparts and white underparts, and a brilliant white rump visible as it flies off with loud, liquid 'tlueet' calls. Like the solitary sandpiper, it nests in abandoned tree nests in boreal forest, a remarkable behaviour for a wader. Breeds across the boreal forests of Scandinavia and Russia east to the Pacific; a winter visitor to waterways across Britain, Africa, and South Asia. One of the earliest autumn wader migrants.

children.name: Tringa glareola
children.description: The most abundant and widespread Tringa wader on passage through Europe. Its spotted brown upperparts, prominent white supercilium, and high-pitched 'chiff-if-if' call make it easy to identify. Breeds in wet forest clearings and boggy terrain throughout boreal Scandinavia and Russia. In autumn, large flocks gather at freshwater wetlands during southward migration, making it one of the most numerous waders at inland sites.

children.name: Tringa brevipes
children.description: A medium-sized, grey-plumaged wader of rocky shores and coral reefs in the Pacific and Indian Oceans, breeding in montane river valleys of eastern Siberia and migrating to the coasts of Australia and Pacific islands. Very similar to the wandering tattler but separable by a whiter supercilium extending behind the eye and a softer, rising 'tu-weet' call. Characteristically bobs its tail as it walks along rocky shorelines.

children.name: Tringa incana
children.description: A grey-plumaged wader of rocky Pacific shores, breeding in mountain streams of Alaska and the Yukon and wintering on ocean island shores from Hawaii to New Zealand. Famous for its extraordinary navigational ability in crossing vast expanses of open Pacific Ocean. A characteristic species of Hawaiian and Pacific island shores in winter, where it bobs its tail rhythmically as it picks invertebrates from the rocks between waves.

children.name: Tringa guttifer
children.description: Named after Alexander von Nordmann, Finnish-German zoologist and palaeontologist. One of the world's most endangered shorebirds, with a global population probably below 1,000 individuals. Breeds in larch forest along the Sea of Okhotsk coast in Russia and migrates to winter in Southeast Asian estuaries, primarily in Thailand and Myanmar. Its critical dependence on a very small number of staging and wintering sites makes it exceptionally vulnerable to habitat loss. Listed as Endangered.

children.name: Actitis
children.description: Common sandpiper and spotted sandpiper — two small, distinctive waders defined by their habit of constantly bobbing the rear of their body up and down as they walk. The common sandpiper is one of the most widespread and familiar waders in the Palearctic, found on the banks and shores of virtually every lake, river, and stream. Both species are characterised by a stiff-winged, low flight interspersed with glides on bowed wings just above the water surface. The two species are closely related but have complementary distributions: hypoleucos in the Old World, macularius in the Americas.

children.name: Actitis hypoleucos
children.description: One of the most widespread small waders, found bobbing along the banks of waterways from western Europe to the Pacific. Its constant tail-bobbing, stiff bowed-wing flight, and shrill piping call are among the most familiar sights and sounds of lakesides and riverbanks in summer. Arrives in northern Europe in late April from African wintering grounds; chicks are precocial and follow parents along the water's edge from their first day.

children.name: Actitis macularius
children.description: The North American counterpart to the common sandpiper, identifiable in breeding plumage by its striking round black spots on clean white underparts. Notable for a polyandrous mating system in which females hold territories and compete for males, while males perform most of the incubation and chick-rearing. Breeds along waterways across North America from Alaska to the southern US; winters from the southern US to central South America. Like its Old World counterpart, it constantly bobs its tail as it walks.

children.name: Calidris
children.description: Stints, sandpipers, and allies — the most diverse genus of small waders, encompassing the majority of the world's diminutive shorebirds and including several larger species such as the knot and the ruff. Calidris species are primarily known as passage migrants and winter visitors to coasts, with a few species breeding in Arctic and subarctic zones. The dunlin is the most abundant small wader on European coasts outside the breeding season; in autumn it forms huge, densely packed flocks that wheel and turn over tidal flats in perfectly synchronised murmurations. Many Calidris species undergo dramatic seasonal plumage changes, appearing strikingly different in breeding and non-breeding seasons.

children.name: Calidris alpina
children.description: The most abundant small wader on European and East Asian coasts, present in huge numbers during spring and autumn migration and in smaller numbers throughout winter. In summer plumage its distinctive black belly patch and rufous back make it striking; in winter it is plain grey above and white below. Autumn flocks at estuaries can reach many thousands of birds, performing spectacular aerial manoeuvres over the tidal flats.

children.name: Calidris canutus
children.description: A medium-sized wader famous for its enormous staging concentrations on coasts during migration, forming vast flocks that wheel and swirl over tidal mudflats. Breeds on high Arctic tundra; coastal mudflats serve as critical refuelling stops between breeding grounds and wintering areas. In spring plumage the rich brick-red underparts are stunning. The size of knot flocks using the Wadden Sea has declined dramatically in recent decades — a major conservation concern linked to food web changes.

children.name: Calidris tenuirostris
children.description: The largest Calidris sandpiper, with a proportionally longer bill than the red knot and heavily spotted brown-and-chestnut breeding plumage. Breeds on high mountain tundra in northeastern Siberia and undertakes one of the most dramatic migrations of any shorebird, staging in enormous numbers on the Yellow Sea mudflats before continuing to wintering areas in Australia. Listed as Endangered, with populations declining precipitously due to reclamation of Yellow Sea intertidal habitats.

children.name: Calidris alba
children.description: The classic 'wave-chaser' of sandy beaches, running at extraordinary speed back and forth in the surf line to snatch invertebrates exposed by each wave. In breeding plumage strikingly rusty red about the head and breast; in winter plain pale grey above and white below, the palest of the Calidris sandpipers. Breeds on high Arctic tundra across the Holarctic and winters on sandy beaches on virtually every coastline in the world — one of the most cosmopolitan of all waders.

children.name: Calidris ferruginea
children.description: An elegant, long-billed sandpiper with a distinctly decurved bill tip and a clean white rump patch visible in flight. In breeding plumage the rich brick-red underparts are as striking as any small wader; in autumn birds are grey above and white below. Breeds on the high Arctic tundra of central Siberia; a long-distance migrant wintering in sub-Saharan Africa and Australia, stopping at European estuaries in large numbers during passage. Populations have declined significantly due to Yellow Sea habitat loss.

children.name: Calidris maritima
children.description: A stocky, dark sandpiper of rocky shores and stony beaches, named for a subtle purplish gloss on its dark winter plumage. One of the hardiest of waders, wintering further north than most other shorebirds on rocky headlands, jetties, and seaweed-covered boulders battered by Atlantic surf. Breeds on Arctic tundra and mountain plateaus from Canada and Greenland across the North Atlantic to Norway and Siberia. A faithful winter visitor to the same rocky headlands year after year.

children.name: Calidris temminckii
children.description: Named after Coenraad Jacob Temminck, the prolific Dutch zoologist and director of the Natural History Museum in Leiden who described hundreds of bird species. A small, unobtrusive stint that is plain mouse-brown above and white below, lacking the prominent supercilium of most small stints. Breeds in northern Scandinavia and across the Siberian taiga to the Arctic, often in drier habitats than other stints. Winters around the Mediterranean and in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Calidris minuta
children.description: The most common small stint on European passage, a tiny, active wader that runs rapidly at the water's edge, picking constantly at small invertebrates. In breeding plumage it shows a striking rufous-and-black pattern; in autumn birds are grey-brown above with a prominent white V on the back. Breeds on high Arctic tundra from Norway east to Siberia; winters in enormous numbers in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Passage flocks at European estuaries can number in the thousands in autumn.

children.name: Calidris minutilla
children.description: The world's smallest shorebird at around 14 cm, a tiny wader with yellow legs that distinguish it from other North American peeps. Breeds in subarctic and boreal marshes, bogs, and tundra across Canada and Alaska; one of the most widespread and abundant shorebirds in the Americas, wintering from the southern US to northern South America. Forages by picking tiny invertebrates from wet mud, often in flocks of hundreds at stopover sites during migration.

children.name: Calidris pusilla
children.description: One of the most abundant shorebirds in North America, staging in enormous concentrations at key migration stopovers such as the Bay of Fundy in Canada, where flocks of hundreds of thousands gather in late summer. Named for the partial webbing between the front toes, visible at close range. Breeds on Arctic tundra across northern Canada and Alaska; winters on Pacific and Atlantic coasts of South America. The semipalmation allows it to wade in slightly deeper water than other small stints.

children.name: Calidris mauri
children.description: Named after Ernesto Mauri, Italian botanist and friend of the describer Charles Lucien Bonaparte. The most abundant shorebird along the Pacific coast of North America during migration, staging in millions along the Pacific flyway. Breeds almost exclusively on the tundra of western Alaska; winters from California and the Gulf coast south to Peru. The slightly drooped bill tip distinguishes it from the semipalmated sandpiper, its closest ecological counterpart. A key species in Pacific flyway conservation.

children.name: Calidris fuscicollis
children.description: A long-winged sandpiper with a distinctive white rump patch that is visible in flight and diagnostic at all seasons. Breeds on Arctic tundra of northern Canada; undertakes one of the longest migrations of any small shorebird, spending the winter on the pampas and coastal wetlands of South America as far south as Tierra del Fuego. In autumn, birds stage in large numbers at coastal wetlands on the Atlantic seaboard before making a direct transatlantic crossing to South America.

children.name: Calidris bairdii
children.description: Named after Spencer Fullerton Baird, the influential Secretary of the Smithsonian Institution and leading figure of 19th-century American natural history. A long-winged, buffy sandpiper that migrates through the interior of North America, preferring drier upland habitats to the muddy shores used by most Calidris species. Breeds on Arctic tundra; migrates to the pampas and Andean altiplano of South America, including some of the highest-altitude wintering areas used by any shorebird.

children.name: Calidris melanotos
children.description: A medium-sized sandpiper with a sharply defined border between the brown-streaked breast and the white belly, as if wearing a bib. Males are notably larger than females and inflate a throat sac during the booming display flight on Arctic breeding grounds. Breeds on tundra across Arctic Canada and Siberia; a long-distance migrant that winters largely in South America and eastern Australia, and is one of the most regular American vagrant waders to appear in Europe each autumn.

children.name: Calidris acuminata
children.description: Named for its pointed tail feathers, this medium-sized sandpiper breeds in wet meadows and boggy tundra of northern Siberia and migrates to eastern Australia, staging in remarkable concentrations on the wetlands of New South Wales and Victoria. In breeding plumage it has rich chestnut tones on the crown and back; in autumn juveniles are attractively marked with orange-buff fringes. One of the most regular trans-equatorial migrants of the east Asian–Australasian flyway.

children.name: Calidris himantopus
children.description: A distinctive, long-legged North American sandpiper with a drooped bill tip that allows it to feed by probing in deeper water than most Calidris species, often up to its belly. In breeding plumage it shows a rufous ear patch and strongly barred underparts; in autumn it is grey above and white below. Breeds on Arctic tundra in northern Canada and Alaska; winters largely in interior South America, particularly in Argentina and Bolivia. Formerly placed in the monotypic genus Micropalama.

children.name: Calidris falcinellus
children.description: A small, distinctive sandpiper with a uniquely kinked, broad-tipped bill and a striking double supercilium in breeding plumage that gives it a puzzled expression. Breeds in boggy habitats in Scandinavia and across Siberia to Japan; winters from the Persian Gulf and East Africa to Australia. The drooped bill tip resembles that of a snipe — formerly placed in the monotypic genus Limicola. Often overlooked among dunlin flocks, but the bill shape and facial pattern are diagnostic.

children.name: Calidris subminuta
children.description: A small, long-toed stint of East Asian wetlands, breeding in boggy forest clearings and wet meadows in Siberia from the Urals east to the Pacific, and wintering in South and Southeast Asia and Australia. The elongated toes are an adaptation for walking on floating vegetation in its preferred freshwater wetland habitats. A frequent migrant at freshwater sites throughout Asia in contrast to most other stints which prefer coastal mudflats.

children.name: Calidris ruficollis
children.description: The most abundant small wader in the East Asian–Australasian flyway, staging in millions at Yellow Sea mudflats during migration between its Siberian breeding grounds and Australian wintering areas. In breeding plumage the bright rusty-orange head and upper breast are distinctive; in winter it resembles other small stints. Breeds on Arctic tundra across northeastern Siberia; like other flyway waders it is threatened by reclamation and degradation of Yellow Sea intertidal habitats.

children.name: Calidris pygmaea
children.description: One of the world's most critically endangered birds, with a global population estimated at only a few hundred individuals and declining sharply. Its extraordinary spatula-shaped bill, unique among waders, is used to sweep from side to side in shallow water and soft mud to detect and capture small invertebrates. Breeds on coastal tundra in the Chukotka Peninsula of northeastern Russia; winters on tidal mudflats in Myanmar and Bangladesh. Conservation efforts include captive breeding and head-starting programmes. Formerly in Eurynorhynchus.

children.name: Calidris virgata
children.description: A stocky, turnstone-like wader of rocky Pacific shores, replacing the purple sandpiper in its ecological niche on the North American Pacific coast. In breeding plumage it shows bold rufous-and-black patterning; in winter it is grey above with a clean white undertail and black-tipped tail. Breeds on high mountain tundra in Alaska and the Yukon; winters on rocky coastlines from Alaska south to southern Chile. Formerly placed in the monotypic genus Aphriza.

children.name: Calidris ptilocnemis
children.description: The North Pacific counterpart of the purple sandpiper, a stocky, dark wader of rocky shores and coastal tundra around the Bering Sea and North Pacific. Breeds on tundra in Alaska, the Aleutian Islands, and the Commander Islands; winters on rocky shores from the Aleutians south to California and Japan. The Pribilof Islands subspecies ptilocnemis has white wingbars and pale underparts in breeding plumage; other subspecies are darker and more richly patterned.

children.name: Calidris subruficollis
children.description: An elegant, buff-washed sandpiper with scaly patterned upperparts and a distinctive small, round head, breeding on dry Arctic tundra and wintering on the pampas of South America. Famous for its remarkable lek display in which males gather on traditional display grounds and show off the silvery-white underwing pattern to females in a dramatic spread-wing display. Formerly placed in the monotypic genus Tryngites. Classified as Near Threatened due to hunting pressure historically and ongoing grassland conversion.

children.name: Calidris pugnax
children.description: One of the most remarkable waders in terms of sexual dimorphism and breeding behaviour. Males in spring grow extravagant ruffs and ear-tufts in an extraordinary variety of colours — from black to white, chestnut to barred — to display at communal lek sites. Three male strategies exist: independent territorial males, satellite males with white ruffs who are tolerated on leks, and rare 'faeder' males who mimic females. Breeds in wet meadows across Scandinavia and Siberia; winters in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Formerly Philomachus pugnax.

children.name: Calidris paramelanotos
children.description: A mysterious hybrid-origin sandpiper, long known only from wintering birds in Australia before its breeding grounds and parentage were unravelled; genetic studies revealed it to be of hybrid origin between pectoral sandpiper and curlew sandpiper. Recorded from coastal wetlands and mudflats in southern Australia; first described formally in 1982 from specimens collected in South Australia. Its uncertain taxonomic status has made it a subject of considerable interest in the ornithological community.

children.name: Arenaria
children.description: Turnstones — two species of stocky, short-billed waders named for their habit of flipping over stones, seaweed, and other debris with rapid sideways movements of the bill to expose small invertebrates sheltering beneath. The ruddy turnstone is one of the most widespread and cosmopolitan waders in the world, wintering on rocky shores on virtually every coastline from the Arctic to the Antarctic. Turnstones are highly adaptable feeders, known to eat carrion, birds' eggs, and even human food when other prey is scarce. The bold black-and-white and chestnut patterning of the breeding plumage ruddy turnstone is one of the most striking of any wader.

children.name: Arenaria interpres
children.description: One of the most cosmopolitan waders in the world, wintering on rocky and sandy shores on virtually every coastline on the planet. Its bold tortoiseshell patterning in breeding plumage — black-and-white head, chestnut back, orange legs — makes it the most easily identified wader in the world. Breeds on Arctic tundra across the Holarctic. Feeds by characteristically flipping stones, shells, and seaweed with rapid jerks of the bill, a method that is both learned by young birds and highly effective.

children.name: Arenaria melanocephala
children.description: A dark, black-and-white turnstone of the North American Pacific coast, replacing the ruddy turnstone on rocky shores from Alaska to Baja California in winter. In breeding plumage it is mostly black with a few white spots on the breast; in winter it is plain dark brownish-black above and white below. Breeds exclusively on the coastal tundra of western Alaska, migrating south along the Pacific coast to winter on rocky headlands, jetties, and kelp-covered reefs.

children.name: Phalaropus
children.description: Phalaropes — three remarkable species that are unique among waders in being strongly pelagic outside the breeding season, spending months on the open ocean. They have lobed toes for swimming, and are famous for their reversed sexual roles: females are more brightly coloured than males and compete for mates, while males perform all incubation and chick-rearing duties. Phalaropes feed by spinning rapidly on the water surface, creating a small vortex that draws invertebrates upward. The red-necked phalarope is one of the most abundant and wide-ranging waders in the world, with populations of millions.

children.name: Phalaropus lobatus
children.description: The most widespread phalarope, breeding across the subarctic zones of both hemispheres and spending the winter spinning on upwellings in the open ocean far from land. The female, in her bright breeding plumage with orange-red neck sides, is more colourful than the male. Breeds at arctic and subarctic wetlands from Iceland and Scandinavia across Siberia and Canada; remarkably, the eastern population winters largely in the Arabian Sea off Oman and Pakistan. Classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Phalaropus fulicarius
children.description: The largest and most pelagic of the phalaropes, spending most of the year on deep oceanic waters, particularly around major ocean upwellings and cold current systems. Breeding females in their full plumage — brick-red body, black cap, and yellow bill — are among the most colourful of all waders. Breeds on high Arctic tundra across the Holarctic; the wide, flat bill (giving the species its name fulicarius, meaning 'coot-like') allows it to filter small organisms from the sea surface.

children.name: Phalaropus tricolor
children.description: Named after Alexander Wilson, the Scottish-American ornithologist. The largest phalarope and the only one that is not pelagic in winter — it winters on salt lakes and coastal lagoons in South America, particularly in Argentina. It has the most striking reversed sexual plumage of the three species, with females in breeding plumage having a vivid chestnut-and-grey face pattern. Breeds in freshwater marshes and lakeshores in the interior of North America; staging flocks at Great Salt Lake in Utah can number in the hundreds of thousands.

children.name: Limnodromus
children.description: Dowitchers — three species of medium-sized, long-billed waders named for their distinctive feeding action of plunging the bill vertically into soft mud in rapid, repeated sewing-machine motions. They combine the elongated bill of a snipe with a plump body and medium-length legs. The two North American species, long-billed and short-billed dowitchers, are among the most challenging identification problems in shorebird watching, being nearly identical in appearance but separable by call. The Asian dowitcher is the most range-restricted of the three, breeding in central Asia and migrating to Southeast Asia.

children.name: Limnodromus griseus
children.description: A medium-sized dowitcher that, despite its name, is not reliably separable from the long-billed dowitcher by bill length alone. The three subspecies show considerable variation and winter plumage birds are virtually identical to long-billed dowitchers; the call — a soft, mellow 'tu tu tu' — is the most reliable distinguishing feature. Breeds in subarctic Canada and Alaska; winters predominantly on coastal mudflats, tidal channels, and mangrove creeks from the southern US to South America.

children.name: Limnodromus scolopaceus
children.description: The longer-billed North American dowitcher that tends to favour freshwater habitats over the coastal mudflats preferred by the short-billed dowitcher in winter. The key call distinction — a sharp, single-note 'keek' unlike the mellow call of the short-billed — is the most reliable separation character. Breeds on Arctic tundra from Alaska to northeastern Siberia; winters from the southern US and Mexico south to Ecuador and Peru, with occasional birds reaching western Europe.

children.name: Limnodromus semipalmatus
children.description: The least-known of the dowitchers, a large species with a distinctive all-black bill that swells at the tip unlike the pale-based bills of North American dowitchers. Breeds in boggy grasslands and lake shores in Mongolia, northern China, and southeast Russia; migrates through China to winter on coastal mudflats of Southeast Asia from Thailand to Borneo. Listed as Near Threatened due to habitat loss at staging sites along the Yellow Sea coast and on Southeast Asian wintering grounds.

children.name: Xenus
children.description: Terek sandpiper — a monotypic genus containing a single distinctive species characterised by its strongly upturned bill and remarkable forward-tilting feeding posture. The terek sandpiper is one of the most active and energetic of all waders, running rapidly along mudflats with its body tilted forward and bill angled upward, in pursuit of fiddler crabs and other small invertebrates. Its name Xenus means 'stranger' or 'alien' in Greek, referring to its unusual bill shape. Despite its odd morphology it is clearly part of the Tringa–Actitis clade within the Scolopacidae.

children.name: Xenus cinereus
children.description: A highly energetic wader with a distinctively upturned bill and bright orange-yellow legs, named after the Terek River in Russia where specimens were first described. Breeds along rivers and lakeshores in the boreal zone from Finland east across Russia to Siberia; a long-distance migrant wintering on tropical mudflats from East Africa to Australia. The characteristic running-and-lunging feeding behaviour — sprinting after crabs across the mud with the bill angled upward — is unmistakeable.

children.name: Bartramia
children.description: Upland sandpiper — a monotypic genus containing one of the most distinctive of all shorebirds, notable for its preference for dry upland grasslands rather than wetlands, its small dove-like head on a long neck, and its haunting, liquid whistling calls. Named after John Bartram, the pioneering American botanist. The upland sandpiper is one of the most important migratory landbirds of the North American interior, undertaking a remarkable migration between its grassland breeding grounds and the South American pampas.

children.name: Bartramia longicauda
children.description: Named for the genus Bartramia, which honours John Bartram, America's first native-born botanist. A large, long-tailed sandpiper of dry grasslands and prairies that is rarely found near water, feeding on grasshoppers, crickets, and other terrestrial invertebrates. The bubbling, liquid 'ip-ip-ip-wheeeeeee-ip-ip' song, delivered during a soaring display flight, is one of the most beautiful and evocative sounds of the North American prairie. A long-distance migrant wintering on the pampas of Argentina and Uruguay.

children.name: Tryngites
children.description: Buff-breasted sandpiper — a monotypic genus (now often included within Calidris) containing a single distinctive sandpiper of dry tundra and grasslands. Sometimes treated as part of Calidris based on molecular phylogenetics, but retained here as a traditional genus. The species is famous for its extraordinary lek display behaviour and for the dramatic sexual dimorphism in size — males considerably larger than females. A bird of open landscapes, it breeds on dry Arctic tundra and winters on the pampas of South America.

children.name: Tryngites subruficollis
children.description: A slim, elegant sandpiper of dry tundra and grasslands with warm buff underparts and scaly patterned upperparts, and a small dove-like head. Males gather at traditional lek sites on the Arctic tundra and display by raising and spreading their wings to show the silvery-white underwing pattern to visiting females. Breeds on dry Arctic tundra in Alaska and northern Canada; a long-distance migrant to the pampas and floodplains of Argentina and Paraguay. Near Threatened due to hunting pressure and grassland conversion.

children.name: Prosobonia
children.description: Pacific sandpipers — a genus of enigmatic small sandpipers restricted to remote Pacific islands, representing the outcome of ancient colonisation events from the wider Scolopacidae. Two living species are recognised, both found in the Tuamotu and Kiribati archipelagos; additional species are known from fossil records on other Pacific islands. These birds evolved in isolation on oceanic atolls, developing adaptations for foraging in coral reef and beach environments far from the continental wetland habitats typical of most scolopacids.

children.name: Prosobonia cancellata
children.description: A small, cryptically patterned sandpiper endemic to low-lying atolls in the Tuamotu Archipelago of French Polynesia, one of the most range-restricted of all waders. Inhabits the coral rubble and beach strand of remote coral atolls, foraging for invertebrates in the wrack line and among rocks. Once found across multiple atolls, it has been extirpated from many islands by introduced rats and now survives on only a handful of rat-free or rat-managed atolls. Classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Prosobonia ellisi
children.description: Named after William Webb Ellis, a 19th-century English clergyman associated with the mythological origins of rugby football. Restricted to a small number of remote atolls in the Phoenix Islands group of Kiribati in the central Pacific. Known from very few specimens and observations; its ecology and behaviour are poorly documented. Like the Tuamotu sandpiper it inhabits coral strand and atoll margins, and faces threats from introduced predators and rising sea levels that threaten its low-lying island habitats.
